topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt cold, sharp against the warmth of my own skin. I stood near the entrance, a knot tightening in my chest as I took in the display. My canvas, vibrant with the captured light of a forgotten afternoon, hung on a stark white wall, framed by other, unfamiliar pieces. A woman with a practiced, distant smile pointed toward my work, her voice smooth as polished glass. "Quite striking," she murmured, her eyes lingering on the texture of the paint. I felt a sudden, heavy pressure behind my eyes, a slow, sinking feeling that made my feet feel rooted to the polished floor. I watched as she circled my painting, her movements detached, treating my expression of the scene like a mere object to be cataloged. A deep sigh escaped me, a sound so quiet it was swallowed by the hushed reverence of the room. I simply stood there, hands clenched at my sides, feeling profoundly small under the weight of their appraisal.

[story 2]

When I found the photographs spread across the communal table, a wave of nausea washed over me. My images—the ones I poured hours into, the raw, unfiltered moments of my life—were there, professionally lit and framed, yet I hadn't even received a single notification. I walked toward the display slowly, my gaze tracing the lines of my own composition. My jaw ached, a dull throb mirroring the ache in my gut. Someone had chosen to place my vulnerability on public view without a word, without a consultation. I leaned in close, my breath catching in my throat. "This isn't right," I managed, the words thin and reedy. The curator merely offered a polite, vacant nod, completely unperturbed by the tremor in my voice. I pulled back, the sensation of being utterly exposed making my skin crawl. I felt a desperate need to shield the fragile thing I had created, a fierce, protective instinct rising up to meet the cold indifference of the space.

[story 3]

The silence in the viewing room was suffocating. I kept glancing toward the corner where my collection was hung, a feeling of profound disorientation settling over me. It wasn't anger that surfaced, not immediately; it was a deep, aching sorrow, like watching a beloved structure slowly crumble. A collector was discussing my abstract pieces, their voices overlapping in a low, self-satisfied murmur. I watched their reactions—the appreciative nods, the subtle adjustments of posture—and felt a strange hollowness bloom in my core. My hands started to tremble, not from fear, but from the sheer injustice of it all. I wanted to scream, to demand that they acknowledge the personal history embedded in every brushstroke, but the sound caught somewhere in my throat, trapped behind a sudden, hot sting of tears. I simply stood, letting the weight of their ownership settle onto my shoulders, a heavy, unwelcome cloak.
